\"Register\") [Login](https://www.ncsl.org/login?returnurl=%2fenvironment-and-natural-resources%2fper-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances)\nSearch Search\n  * [Resources](https://www.ncsl.org/our-work)\n  * [News](https://www.ncsl.org/news)\n  * [Events](https://www.ncsl.org/events)\n  * [About Us](https://www.ncsl.org/about-us)\n\n#  Report Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS) | State Legislation and Federal Action \n![Hand of scientist holding flask with lab glassware and test tubes](https://images.ncsl.org/image/upload/t_Horizontal_750/c_fill,g_auto,w_600/f_auto,q_auto/v1668106574/website/GettyImages-1169091429.jpg)\nUpdated February 13, 2026\nA **Report** is an in-depth look at a topic.\nRelated Topic: [Environment and Natural Resources](https://www.ncsl.org/ncsl-search-results?topics=25)\nExplore key content...\nJump to Section... \n[Federal Action](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#fed)\n[State Action](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#state)\n[PFAS in Drinking Water](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#water)\n[PFAS in Firefighting Foam and Personal Protective Equipment](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#fire)\n[PFAS Cleanup and Remediation](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#cleanup)\n[PFAS in Packaging and Consumer Products](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#packaging)\n### Key Takeaways\n  * PFAS describes a group of synthetic chemicals found in many products we use daily. They are often referred to as “forever chemicals” because they do not break down naturally. PFAS have been detected in humans, soil, water and air.\n  * States are using multipronged approaches to manage PFAS: reducing or eliminating their use in common source material, setting testing and reporting limits, and directing and financing remediation.\n  * Ongoing studies continue to expand our understanding of how PFAS impact both human health and the environment. Research indicates that long-term exposure to elevated levels of certain PFAS may be associated with health concerns.\n  * The federal government has set maximum contaminant levels (MCLs) for a few select PFAS chemicals and offer various funding mechanisms to assist states with management.\n\n## Introduction\n[Per- and poly-fluoroalkyls](https://www.epa.gov/pfas), known more commonly as PFAS, are a group of human-made chemicals not found naturally in the environment that have emerged in the last decade as an issue of increasing interest to states, tribes and the federal government. The PFAS group includes synthetic chemicals such as perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorooctanesulfonic acid (PFOS), GenX and nearly [15,000 others](https://comptox.epa.gov/dashboard/chemical-lists/PFASSTRUCT).\nPFAS are used in a wide range of products used daily—from cookware to rain jackets to pizza boxes—because of their ability to repel dirt, water and grease. Since their introduction in the 1940s, the chemicals have been used in a variety of industries and consumer goods, including carpeting, paper packaging, nonstick and weather-resistant coatings rubber and plastics. As such, most people have been exposed to PFAS at some point in their lives, and while PFOA and PFOS are no longer manufactured in the United States, these chemicals are still produced internationally and imported to the U.S. in consumer products.\nPFAS are known as “forever chemicals” because they are stable and persistent in the environment and in the human body, meaning they do not break down and can accumulate over time. These chemicals are often found in soil and water near sites where they were manufactured, used or discarded. The chemical chains can travel long distances, move through the soil, seep into groundwater or be carried through the air, making them especially difficult to contain.\nScientific studies have shown that exposure to certain levels of PFAS may lead to adverse health effects. According to the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR), studies indicate PFAS can impact responses to vaccines, cholesterol levels, liver and kidney function and pregnancy-induced hypertension and preeclampsia. Additionally, PFAS can affect birth weight, birth defects, delayed development and newborn deaths in laboratory animals.\nGiven the potential health impacts, state legislatures and the federal government are working with communities to advance remediation and cleanup efforts, increase PFAS measurement collection and reduce PFAS discharges.\nRead More\n##  Federal Action\n### A Timeline of EPA Actions\nIn 2016, the Environmental Protection Agency published a non-enforceable health advisory on PFOA and PFOS. This [advisory](https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/2016-05/documents/drinkingwaterhealthadvisories_pfoa_pfos_5_19_16.final_.1.pdf)[National Primary Drinking Water Regulation](https://www.epa.gov/sdwa/and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances-pfas), established safe levels of the chemicals in drinking water at no more than 70 parts per trillion.\nIn 2019, the EPA released a formal [PFAS Action Plan](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2019-02/documents/pfas_action_plan_021319_508compliant_1.pdf) which outlined long- and short-term previous, ongoing, and anticipated actions. These actions included but were not limited to: developing a maximum containment level for states and local water utilities via the [Safe Drinking Water Act;](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-safe-drinking-water-act) listing PFOA and PFOS as hazardous substances under the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation and Liability Act (CERCLA-also known as Superfund); and developing new and better methods to detect the chemicals in drinking water, soil and groundwater. The EPA updated the plan in [2020](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2020-01/documents/pfas_action_plan_feb2020.pdf), and shared federal actions addressing and mitigating PFAS chemicals in the environment.\nIn 2021, the agency released a new plan, the [PFAS Strategic Roadmap: EPA's Commitments to Action 2021-2024](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2021-10/pfas-roadmap_final-508.pdf), which highlighted new actions, goals and objectives for addressing PFAS at the federal level. Many of these actions have since been completed and are described in more detail in the subsection below. Other objectives achieved, but not described below, include but are not limited to:\n  * Providing [grant funding](https://www.epa.gov/research-grants/research-grants-understanding-pfas-uptake-and-bioaccumulation-plants-and-animals) to support research and management of PFAS in the agriculture sector\n  * New rules that bar federal agencies from purchasing supplies containing PFAS\n  * Adding seven PFAS to the [Toxic Releases Inventory](https://www.epa.gov/toxics-release-inventory-tri-program/tri-listed-chemicals) (TRI) in 2024 and nine in 2025\n  * Publishing [recommendations](https://www.epa.gov/pfas/interim-recommendations-addressing-groundwater-contaminated-pfoa-and-pfos) for groundwater cleanup\n  * Announcing new testing methods for measuring PFAS in [soil](https://www.epa.gov/cwa-methods/cwa-analytical-methods-and-polyfluorinated-alkyl-substances-pfas), [air](https://www.epa.gov/emc/emc-other-test-methods), water systems, and [wastewater, including](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-01/method-1621-for-web-posting.pdf) methodology for [11](https://www.epa.gov/newsreleases/epa-announces-new-method-test-additional-pfas-drinking-water) additional types of PFAS in drinking water\n\n### Recent Actions\n#### 2024\nThe EPA took several significant steps to address PFAS in 2024 and continues its work on PFAS.\n  * Finalized the [National Primary Drinking Water Regulation](https://www.epa.gov/sdwa/and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances-pfas), which established the first legally enforceable federal levels—also known as maximum contaminant levels (MCLs)—for six per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances chemicals. However, in 2025, four of these were removed for review: perfluorohexane sulfonic acid (PFHxS), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluorobutane sulfonate (PFBS), and HFPO-DA more commonly known as GenX chemicals. The enforceable [MCL](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-04/pfas-npdwr_fact-sheet_general_4.9.24v1.pdf) 's for PFOA and PFOS at 4.0 parts per trillion remain in place. This regulation also requires public water systems to monitor PFAS levels in drinking water, notify the public of those levels and reduce the levels if they exceed the federal standards. It also preempts previously established state drinking water regulations or guidance values to ensure compliance with the minimum federal levels.\n  * [Designated](https://www."

PFAS Chain Length Impacts on Regulation and Treatment for Landfill Leachate\nBy: [Lingke Zeng, LEED AP](https://www.sanbornhead.com/news-insights/?by-author=2543) and [Steve Zemba, PhD, PE](https://www.sanbornhead.com/news-insights/?by-author=1007) on August 24, 2022 \n![](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/AdobeStock_429831073-675x490.jpeg)\nPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, known as PFAS, are the most well-known emerging contaminants and have become an urgent environmental issue in recent years. PFAS have been detected in [landfill leachate](https://www.sanbornhead.com/keep-those-landfill-liquids-flowing/) at levels above drinking water standards. Although PFAS treatment is not currently required for landfill leachate, the progress in PFAS regulation at the national level suggests that PFAS standards in surface water will be established and landfill leachate treatment will be required.\nAs landfill operators contemplate changes in environmental regulations, it should be noted that not all PFAS contaminants behave in the same way. In fact, examining the compound chain length of PFAS will help us to identify which compounds require additional treatment to eliminate their impacts on the environment.\n## PFAS **Chain Length Impact on Contamination**\nBased on the carbon chain number, perfluoroalkyl acids (PFAAs) can be divided into short-chain and long-chain compounds, as shown in Table 1. The classification of short- and long-chain PFAS is based on the bioaccumulation potential of the individual compound. The long-chain PFAS typically show high bioaccumulation potential. For perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs), with a carboxylic function head, the long-chain PFAS include compounds with a carbon number of more than seven. For perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs), with a sulfonic function head, the long-chain PFAS include compounds with a carbon number of more than five. The chain definition of polyfluoroalkyl substances is not well defined but generally follows a similar trend as PFAAs. \n![Table showing short- and long-chain PFAA classifications from ITRC Regulatory Framework for PFAS](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Table-1-recreated-1024x160.png)Table 1: PFAA short- and long-chain classification (Source from [ITRC Regulatory Framework for PFAS released in February 2021](https://pfas-1.itrcweb.org/))\n## **Landfill Leachate Composition**\nHalf of the total PFAS in landfill leachate can be contributed by polyfluoroalkyl substances, especially 5:3 Fluorotelomer carboxylic acid (5:3 FTCA) – a compound not on most analytical lists. However, typically only PFAAs are regulated and most landfills have been collecting PFAAs and a limited number of polyfluoroalkyl substances. Figure 1 demonstrates the PFAAs composition in an example landfill. At either the upper or lower limits of the total concentration range, the short-chain PFAS are the dominant group. Although perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA) is listed as short-chain in Table 1, its characteristic is in-line with long-chain and grouped with long-chain PFAS.\n![Charts comparing high and low concentrations of PFAS composition in leachate at an example landfill](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Figure1-1024x403.png)Figure 1: PFAS Composition at an example landfill.  \nLeft – Composition at lowest combined concentration; Right – Composition at highest combined concentration\n## PFAS **Chain Length on Chemical and Physical Characteristics**\nAll PFAS, being surfactants, contain a hydrophobic tail and a hydrophilic function head. Long-chain PFAS show more hydrophobicity (dislike water) and short-chain PFAS show more hydrophilicity (like water). Therefore,\n  * Higher solubility is often observed for a compound with high hydrophilicity. Solubility typically increases when the carbon chain number decreases. \n  * Sorption efficiency is high for a compound with high hydrophobicity. Sorption typically increases when the carbon chain number increases.\n  * Surfactants can form foam when gas is applied to the water. The ability to form foam increases when the carbon chain number increases. Short-chain PFAS are less effective at forming foams. \n  * PFAS also demonstrate volatility and short-chain PFAS can have higher volatility.\n\n## **Impact on Regulation**\nA number of states have established groundwater and drinking water s",

Legislation and Federal Action \n![Hand of scientist holding flask with lab glassware and test Products](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#packaging)\n### Key Takeaways\n  * PFAS describes a group of synthetic chemicals found in many products we use daily. They are often referred to as “forever chemicals” because they do not break down naturally. PFAS have been detected in humans, soil, water and air.\n  * States are using multipronged approaches to manage PFAS: reducing or eliminating their use in common source material, setting testing and reporting limits, and directing and financing remediation.\n  * Ongoing studies continue to expand our understanding of how PFAS impact both human health and the environment. Research indicates that long-term exposure to elevated levels of certain PFAS may be associated with health concerns.\n  * The federal government has set maximum contaminant levels (MCLs) for a few select PFAS chemicals and offer various funding mechanisms to assist states with management.\n\n## Introduction\n[Per- and poly-fluoroalkyls](https://www.epa.gov/pfas), known more commonly as PFAS, are a group of human-made chemicals not found naturally in the environment that have emerged in the last decade as an issue of increasing interest to states, tribes and the federal government. The PFAS group includes synthetic chemicals such as perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorooctanesulfonic acid (PFOS), GenX and nearly [15,000 others](https://comptox.epa.gov/dashboard/chemical-lists/PFASSTRUCT).\nPFAS are used in a wide range of products used daily—from cookware to rain jackets to pizza boxes—because of their ability to repel dirt, water and grease. Since their introduction in the 1940s, the chemicals have been used in a variety of industries and consumer goods, including carpeting, paper packaging, nonstick and weather-resistant coatings rubber and plastics. As such, most people have been exposed to PFAS at some point in their lives, and while PFOA and PFOS are no longer manufactured in the United States, these chemicals are still produced internationally and imported to the U.S. in consumer products.\nPFAS are known as “forever chemicals” because they are stable and persistent in the environment and in the human body, meaning they do not break down and can accumulate over time. These chemicals are often found in soil and water near sites where they were manufactured, used or discarded. The chemical chains can travel long distances, move through the soil, seep into groundwater or be carried through the air, making them especially difficult to contain.\nScientific studies have shown that exposure to certain levels of PFAS may lead to adverse health effects. According to the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR), studies indicate PFAS can impact responses to vaccines, cholesterol levels, liver and kidney function and pregnancy-induced hypertension and preeclampsia. Additionally, PFAS can affect birth weight, birth defects, delayed development and newborn deaths in laboratory animals.\nGiven the potential health impacts, state legislatures and the federal government are working with communities to advance remediation and cleanup efforts, increase PFAS measurement collection and reduce PFAS discharges.\nRead More\n##  Federal Action\n### A Timeline of EPA Actions\nIn 2016, the Environmental Protection Agency published a non-enforceable health advisory on PFOA and PFOS. This [advisory](https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/2016-05/documents/drinkingwaterhealthadvisories_pfoa_pfos_5_19_16.final_.1.pdf)[National Primary Drinking Water Regulation](https://www.epa.gov/sdwa/and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances-pfas), established safe levels of the chemicals in drinking water at no more than 70 parts per trillion.\nIn 2019, the EPA released a formal [PFAS Action Plan](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2019-02/documents/pfas_action_plan_021319_508compliant_1.pdf) which outlined long- and short-term previous, ongoing, and anticipated actions. These actions included but were not limited to: developing a maximum containment level for states and local water utilities via the [Safe Drinking Water Act;](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-safe-drinking-water-act) listing PFOA and PFOS as hazardous substances under the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation and Liability Act (CERCLA-also known as Superfund); and developing new and better methods to detect the chemicals in drinking water, soil and groundwater. The EPA updated the plan in [2020](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2020-01/documents/pfas_action_plan_feb2020.pdf), and shared federal actions addressing and mitigating PFAS chemicals in the environment.\nIn 2021, the agency released a new plan, the [PFAS Strategic Roadmap: EPA's Commitments to Action 2021-2024](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2021-10/pfas-roadmap_final-508.pdf), which highlighted new actions, goals and objectives for addressing PFAS at the federal level. Many of these actions have since been completed and are described in more detail in the subsection below. Other objectives achieved, but not described below, include but are not limited to:\n  * Providing [grant funding](https://www.epa.gov/research-grants/research-grants-understanding-pfas-uptake-and-bioaccumulation-plants-and-animals) to support research and management of PFAS in the agriculture sector\n  * New rules that bar federal agencies from purchasing supplies containing PFAS\n  * Adding seven PFAS to the [Toxic Releases Inventory](https://www.epa.gov/toxics-release-inventory-tri-program/tri-listed-chemicals) (TRI) in 2024 and nine in 2025\n  * Publishing [recommendations](https://www.epa.gov/pfas/interim-recommendations-addressing-groundwater-contaminated-pfoa-and-pfos) for groundwater cleanup\n  * Announcing new testing methods for measuring PFAS in [soil](https://www.epa.gov/cwa-methods/cwa-analytical-methods-and-polyfluorinated-alkyl-substances-pfas), [air](https://www.epa.gov/emc/emc-other-test-methods), water systems, and [wastewater, including](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-01/method-1621-for-web-posting.pdf) methodology for [11](https://www.epa.gov/newsreleases/epa-announces-new-method-test-additional-pfas-drinking-water) additional types of PFAS in drinking water\n\n### Recent Actions\n#### 2024\nThe EPA took several significant steps to address PFAS in 2024 and continues its work on PFAS.\n  * Finalized the [National Primary Drinking Water Regulation](https://www.epa.gov/sdwa/and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances-pfas), which established the first legally enforceable federal levels—also known as maximum contaminant levels (MCLs)—for six per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances chemicals. However, in 2025, four of these were removed for review: perfluorohexane sulfonic acid (PFHxS), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluorobutane sulfonate (PFBS), and HFPO-DA more commonly known as GenX chemicals. The enforceable [MCL](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-04/pfas-npdwr_fact-sheet_general_4.9.24v1.pdf) 's for PFOA and PFOS at 4.0 parts per trillion remain in place. This regulation also requires public water systems to monitor PFAS levels in drinking water, notify the public of those levels and reduce the levels if they exceed the federal standards. It also preempts previously established state drinking water regulations or guidance values to ensure compliance with the minimum federal levels.\n  * [Designated](https://www.",

\"Register\") [Login](https://www.ncsl.org/login?returnurl=%2fenvironment-and-natural-resources%2fper-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances)\nSearch Search\n  * [Resources](https://www.ncsl.org/our-work)\n  * [News](https://www.ncsl.org/news)\n  * [Events](https://www.ncsl.org/events)\n  * [About Us](https://www.ncsl.org/about-us)\n\n#  Report Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS) | State Legislation and Federal Action \n![Hand of scientist holding flask with lab glassware and test tubes](https://images.ncsl.org/image/upload/t_Horizontal_750/c_fill,g_auto,w_600/f_auto,q_auto/v1668106574/website/GettyImages-1169091429.jpg)\nUpdated February 13, 2026\nA **Report** is an in-depth look at a topic.\nRelated Topic: [Environment and Natural Resources](https://www.ncsl.org/ncsl-search-results?topics=25)\nExplore key content...\nJump to Section... \n[Federal Action](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#fed)\n[State Action](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#state)\n[PFAS in Drinking Water](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#water)\n[PFAS in Firefighting Foam and Personal Protective Equipment](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#fire)\n[PFAS Cleanup and Remediation](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#cleanup)\n[PFAS in Packaging and Consumer Products](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#packaging)\n### Key Takeaways\n  * PFAS describes a group of synthetic chemicals found in many products we use daily. They are often referred to as “forever chemicals” because they do not break down naturally. PFAS have been detected in humans, soil, water and air.\n  * States are using multipronged approaches to manage PFAS: reducing or eliminating their use in common source material, setting testing and reporting limits, and directing and financing remediation.\n  * Ongoing studies continue to expand our understanding of how PFAS impact both human health and the environment. Research indicates that long-term exposure to elevated levels of certain PFAS may be associated with health concerns.\n  * The federal government has set maximum contaminant levels (MCLs) for a few select PFAS chemicals and offer various funding mechanisms to assist states with management.\n\n## Introduction\n[Per- and poly-fluoroalkyls](https://www.epa.gov/pfas), known more commonly as PFAS, are a group of human-made chemicals not found naturally in the environment that have emerged in the last decade as an issue of increasing interest to states, tribes and the federal government. The PFAS group includes synthetic chemicals such as perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorooctanesulfonic acid (PFOS), GenX and nearly [15,000 others](https://comptox.epa.gov/dashboard/chemical-lists/PFASSTRUCT).\nPFAS are used in a wide range of products used daily—from cookware to rain jackets to pizza boxes—because of their ability to repel dirt, water and grease. Since their introduction in the 1940s, the chemicals have been used in a variety of industries and consumer goods, including carpeting, paper packaging, nonstick and weather-resistant coatings rubber and plastics. As such, most people have been exposed to PFAS at some point in their lives, and while PFOA and PFOS are no longer manufactured in the United States, these chemicals are still produced internationally and imported to the U.S. in consumer products.\nPFAS are known as “forever chemicals” because they are stable and persistent in the environment and in the human body, meaning they do not break down and can accumulate over time. These chemicals are often found in soil and water near sites where they were manufactured, used or discarded. The chemical chains can travel long distances, move through the soil, seep into groundwater or be carried through the air, making them especially difficult to contain.\nScientific studies have shown that exposure to certain levels of PFAS may lead to adverse health effects. According to the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR), studies indicate PFAS can impact responses to vaccines, cholesterol levels, liver and kidney function and pregnancy-induced hypertension and preeclampsia. Additionally, PFAS can affect birth weight, birth defects, delayed development and newborn deaths in laboratory animals.\nGiven the potential health impacts, state legislatures and the federal government are working with communities to advance remediation and cleanup efforts, increase PFAS measurement collection and reduce PFAS discharges.\nRead More\n##  Federal Action\n### A Timeline of EPA Actions\nIn 2016, the Environmental Protection Agency published a non-enforceable health advisory on PFOA and PFOS. This [advisory](https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/2016-05/documents/drinkingwaterhealthadvisories_pfoa_pfos_5_19_16.final_.1.pdf)[National Primary Drinking Water Regulation](https://www.epa.gov/sdwa/and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances-pfas), established safe levels of the chemicals in drinking water at no more than 70 parts per trillion.\nIn 2019, the EPA released a formal [PFAS Action Plan](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2019-02/documents/pfas_action_plan_021319_508compliant_1.pdf) which outlined long- and short-term previous, ongoing, and anticipated actions. These actions included but were not limited to: developing a maximum containment level for states and local water utilities via the [Safe Drinking Water Act;](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-safe-drinking-water-act) listing PFOA and PFOS as hazardous substances under the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation and Liability Act (CERCLA-also known as Superfund); and developing new and better methods to detect the chemicals in drinking water, soil and groundwater. The EPA updated the plan in [2020](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2020-01/documents/pfas_action_plan_feb2020.pdf), and shared federal actions addressing and mitigating PFAS chemicals in the environment.\nIn 2021, the agency released a new plan, the [PFAS Strategic Roadmap: EPA's Commitments to Action 2021-2024](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2021-10/pfas-roadmap_final-508.pdf), which highlighted new actions, goals and objectives for addressing PFAS at the federal level. Many of these actions have since been completed and are described in more detail in the subsection below. Other objectives achieved, but not described below, include but are not limited to:\n  * Providing [grant funding](https://www.epa.gov/research-grants/research-grants-understanding-pfas-uptake-and-bioaccumulation-plants-and-animals) to support research and management of PFAS in the agriculture sector\n  * New rules that bar federal agencies from purchasing supplies containing PFAS\n  * Adding seven PFAS to the [Toxic Releases Inventory](https://www.epa.gov/toxics-release-inventory-tri-program/tri-listed-chemicals) (TRI) in 2024 and nine in 2025\n  * Publishing [recommendations](https://www.epa.gov/pfas/interim-recommendations-addressing-groundwater-contaminated-pfoa-and-pfos) for groundwater cleanup\n  * Announcing new testing methods for measuring PFAS in [soil](https://www.epa.gov/cwa-methods/cwa-analytical-methods-and-polyfluorinated-alkyl-substances-pfas), [air](https://www.epa.gov/emc/emc-other-test-methods), water systems, and [wastewater, including](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-01/method-1621-for-web-posting.pdf) methodology for [11](https://www.epa.gov/newsreleases/epa-announces-new-method-test-additional-pfas-drinking-water) additional types of PFAS in drinking water\n\n### Recent Actions\n#### 2024\nThe EPA took several significant steps to address PFAS in 2024 and continues its work on PFAS.\n  * Finalized the [National Primary Drinking Water Regulation](https://www.epa.gov/sdwa/and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances-pfas), which established the first legally enforceable federal levels—also known as maximum contaminant levels (MCLs)—for six per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances chemicals. However, in 2025, four of these were removed for review: perfluorohexane sulfonic acid (PFHxS), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluorobutane sulfonate (PFBS), and HFPO-DA more commonly known as GenX chemicals. The enforceable [MCL](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-04/pfas-npdwr_fact-sheet_general_4.9.24v1.pdf) 's for PFOA and PFOS at 4.0 parts per trillion remain in place. This regulation also requires public water systems to monitor PFAS levels in drinking water, notify the public of those levels and reduce the levels if they exceed the federal standards. It also preempts previously established state drinking water regulations or guidance values to ensure compliance with the minimum federal levels.\n  * [Designated](https://www.",

PFAS Chain Length Impacts on Regulation and Treatment for Landfill Leachate\nBy: [Lingke Zeng, LEED AP](https://www.sanbornhead.com/news-insights/?by-author=2543) and [Steve Zemba, PhD, PE](https://www.sanbornhead.com/news-insights/?by-author=1007) on August 24, 2022 \n![](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/AdobeStock_429831073-675x490.jpeg)\nPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, known as PFAS, are the most well-known emerging contaminants and have become an urgent environmental issue in recent years. PFAS have been detected in [landfill leachate](https://www.sanbornhead.com/keep-those-landfill-liquids-flowing/) at levels above drinking water standards. Although PFAS treatment is not currently required for landfill leachate, the progress in PFAS regulation at the national level suggests that PFAS standards in surface water will be established and landfill leachate treatment will be required.\nAs landfill operators contemplate changes in environmental regulations, it should be noted that not all PFAS contaminants behave in the same way. In fact, examining the compound chain length of PFAS will help us to identify which compounds require additional treatment to eliminate their impacts on the environment.\n## PFAS **Chain Length Impact on Contamination**\nBased on the carbon chain number, perfluoroalkyl acids (PFAAs) can be divided into short-chain and long-chain compounds, as shown in Table 1. The classification of short- and long-chain PFAS is based on the bioaccumulation potential of the individual compound. The long-chain PFAS typically show high bioaccumulation potential. For perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs), with a carboxylic function head, the long-chain PFAS include compounds with a carbon number of more than seven. For perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs), with a sulfonic function head, the long-chain PFAS include compounds with a carbon number of more than five. The chain definition of polyfluoroalkyl substances is not well defined but generally follows a similar trend as PFAAs. \n![Table showing short- and long-chain PFAA classifications from ITRC Regulatory Framework for PFAS](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Table-1-recreated-1024x160.png)Table 1: PFAA short- and long-chain classification (Source from [ITRC Regulatory Framework for PFAS released in February 2021](https://pfas-1.itrcweb.org/))\n## **Landfill Leachate Composition**\nHalf of the total PFAS in landfill leachate can be contributed by polyfluoroalkyl substances, especially 5:3 Fluorotelomer carboxylic acid (5:3 FTCA) – a compound not on most analytical lists. However, typically only PFAAs are regulated and most landfills have been collecting PFAAs and a limited number of polyfluoroalkyl substances. Figure 1 demonstrates the PFAAs composition in an example landfill. At either the upper or lower limits of the total concentration range, the short-chain PFAS are the dominant group. Although perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA) is listed as short-chain in Table 1, its characteristic is in-line with long-chain and grouped with long-chain PFAS.\n![Charts comparing high and low concentrations of PFAS composition in leachate at an example landfill](https://www.sanbornhead.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Figure1-1024x403.png)Figure 1: PFAS Composition at an example landfill.  \nLeft – Composition at lowest combined concentration; Right – Composition at highest combined concentration\n## PFAS **Chain Length on Chemical and Physical Characteristics**\nAll PFAS, being surfactants, contain a hydrophobic tail and a hydrophilic function head. Long-chain PFAS show more hydrophobicity (dislike water) and short-chain PFAS show more hydrophilicity (like water). Therefore,\n  * Higher solubility is often observed for a compound with high hydrophilicity. Solubility typically increases when the carbon chain number decreases. \n  * Sorption efficiency is high for a compound with high hydrophobicity. Sorption typically increases when the carbon chain number increases.\n  * Surfactants can form foam when gas is applied to the water. The ability to form foam increases when the carbon chain number increases. Short-chain PFAS are less effective at forming foams. \n  * PFAS also demonstrate volatility and short-chain PFAS can have higher volatility.\n\n## **Impact on Regulation**\nA number of states have established groundwater and drinking water s",

\"Register\") [Login](https://www.ncsl.org/login?returnurl=%2fenvironment-and-natural-resources%2fper-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances)\nSearch Search\n  * [Resources](https://www.ncsl.org/our-work)\n  * [News](https://www.ncsl.org/news)\n  * [Events](https://www.ncsl.org/events)\n  * [About Us](https://www.ncsl.org/about-us)\n\n#  Report Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS) | State Legislation and Federal Action \n![Hand of scientist holding flask with lab glassware and test tubes](https://images.ncsl.org/image/upload/t_Horizontal_750/c_fill,g_auto,w_600/f_auto,q_auto/v1668106574/website/GettyImages-1169091429.jpg)\nUpdated February 13, 2026\nA **Report** is an in-depth look at a topic.\nRelated Topic: [Environment and Natural Resources](https://www.ncsl.org/ncsl-search-results?topics=25)\nExplore key content...\nJump to Section... \n[Federal Action](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#fed)\n[State Action](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#state)\n[PFAS in Drinking Water](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#water)\n[PFAS in Firefighting Foam and Personal Protective Equipment](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#fire)\n[PFAS Cleanup and Remediation](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#cleanup)\n[PFAS in Packaging and Consumer Products](https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances#packaging)\n### Key Takeaways\n  * PFAS describes a group of synthetic chemicals found in many products we use daily. They are often referred to as “forever chemicals” because they do not break down naturally. PFAS have been detected in humans, soil, water and air.\n  * States are using multipronged approaches to manage PFAS: reducing or eliminating their use in common source material, setting testing and reporting limits, and directing and financing remediation.\n  * Ongoing studies continue to expand our understanding of how PFAS impact both human health and the environment. Research indicates that long-term exposure to elevated levels of certain PFAS may be associated with health concerns.\n  * The federal government has set maximum contaminant levels (MCLs) for a few select PFAS chemicals and offer various funding mechanisms to assist states with management.\n\n## Introduction\n[Per- and poly-fluoroalkyls](https://www.epa.gov/pfas), known more commonly as PFAS, are a group of human-made chemicals not found naturally in the environment that have emerged in the last decade as an issue of increasing interest to states, tribes and the federal government. The PFAS group includes synthetic chemicals such as perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorooctanesulfonic acid (PFOS), GenX and nearly [15,000 others](https://comptox.epa.gov/dashboard/chemical-lists/PFASSTRUCT).\nPFAS are used in a wide range of products used daily—from cookware to rain jackets to pizza boxes—because of their ability to repel dirt, water and grease. Since their introduction in the 1940s, the chemicals have been used in a variety of industries and consumer goods, including carpeting, paper packaging, nonstick and weather-resistant coatings rubber and plastics. As such, most people have been exposed to PFAS at some point in their lives, and while PFOA and PFOS are no longer manufactured in the United States, these chemicals are still produced internationally and imported to the U.S. in consumer products.\nPFAS are known as “forever chemicals” because they are stable and persistent in the environment and in the human body, meaning they do not break down and can accumulate over time. These chemicals are often found in soil and water near sites where they were manufactured, used or discarded. The chemical chains can travel long distances, move through the soil, seep into groundwater or be carried through the air, making them especially difficult to contain.\nScientific studies have shown that exposure to certain levels of PFAS may lead to adverse health effects. According to the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR), studies indicate PFAS can impact responses to vaccines, cholesterol levels, liver and kidney function and pregnancy-induced hypertension and preeclampsia. Additionally, PFAS can affect birth weight, birth defects, delayed development and newborn deaths in laboratory animals.\nGiven the potential health impacts, state legislatures and the federal government are working with communities to advance remediation and cleanup efforts, increase PFAS measurement collection and reduce PFAS discharges.\nRead More\n##  Federal Action\n### A Timeline of EPA Actions\nIn 2016, the Environmental Protection Agency published a non-enforceable health advisory on PFOA and PFOS. This [advisory](https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/2016-05/documents/drinkingwaterhealthadvisories_pfoa_pfos_5_19_16.final_.1.pdf)[National Primary Drinking Water Regulation](https://www.epa.gov/sdwa/and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances-pfas), established safe levels of the chemicals in drinking water at no more than 70 parts per trillion.\nIn 2019, the EPA released a formal [PFAS Action Plan](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2019-02/documents/pfas_action_plan_021319_508compliant_1.pdf) which outlined long- and short-term previous, ongoing, and anticipated actions. These actions included but were not limited to: developing a maximum containment level for states and local water utilities via the [Safe Drinking Water Act;](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-safe-drinking-water-act) listing PFOA and PFOS as hazardous substances under the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation and Liability Act (CERCLA-also known as Superfund); and developing new and better methods to detect the chemicals in drinking water, soil and groundwater. The EPA updated the plan in [2020](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2020-01/documents/pfas_action_plan_feb2020.pdf), and shared federal actions addressing and mitigating PFAS chemicals in the environment.\nIn 2021, the agency released a new plan, the [PFAS Strategic Roadmap: EPA's Commitments to Action 2021-2024](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2021-10/pfas-roadmap_final-508.pdf), which highlighted new actions, goals and objectives for addressing PFAS at the federal level. Many of these actions have since been completed and are described in more detail in the subsection below. Other objectives achieved, but not described below, include but are not limited to:\n  * Providing [grant funding](https://www.epa.gov/research-grants/research-grants-understanding-pfas-uptake-and-bioaccumulation-plants-and-animals) to support research and management of PFAS in the agriculture sector\n  * New rules that bar federal agencies from purchasing supplies containing PFAS\n  * Adding seven PFAS to the [Toxic Releases Inventory](https://www.epa.gov/toxics-release-inventory-tri-program/tri-listed-chemicals) (TRI) in 2024 and nine in 2025\n  * Publishing [recommendations](https://www.epa.gov/pfas/interim-recommendations-addressing-groundwater-contaminated-pfoa-and-pfos) for groundwater cleanup\n  * Announcing new testing methods for measuring PFAS in [soil](https://www.epa.gov/cwa-methods/cwa-analytical-methods-and-polyfluorinated-alkyl-substances-pfas), [air](https://www.epa.gov/emc/emc-other-test-methods), water systems, and [wastewater, including](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-01/method-1621-for-web-posting.pdf) methodology for [11](https://www.epa.gov/newsreleases/epa-announces-new-method-test-additional-pfas-drinking-water) additional types of PFAS in drinking water\n\n### Recent Actions\n#### 2024\nThe EPA took several significant steps to address PFAS in 2024 and continues its work on PFAS.\n  * Finalized the [National Primary Drinking Water Regulation](https://www.epa.gov/sdwa/and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances-pfas), which established the first legally enforceable federal levels—also known as maximum contaminant levels (MCLs)—for six per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances chemicals. However, in 2025, four of these were removed for review: perfluorohexane sulfonic acid (PFHxS), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluorobutane sulfonate (PFBS), and HFPO-DA more commonly known as GenX chemicals. The enforceable [MCL](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-04/pfas-npdwr_fact-sheet_general_4.9.24v1.pdf) 's for PFOA and PFOS at 4.0 parts per trillion remain in place. This regulation also requires public water systems to monitor PFAS levels in drinking water, notify the public of those levels and reduce the levels if they exceed the federal standards. It also preempts previously established state drinking water regulations or guidance values to ensure compliance with the minimum federal levels.\n  * [Designated](https://www.",

\"Share this\")\nPolyethyleneimine (PEI) is one of the most versatile cationic polymers used in multiple industries, including water treatment, coatings, paper, textiles, and biotechnology. As a leading polyethyleneimine manufacturer in China, we focus on providing consistent quality, competitive polyethyleneimine price, and reliable technical support to meet global customers’ evolving needs.\n###  ****I********. What Is Polyethyleneimine (PEI)?****\n[Polyethyleneimine (CAS 9002-98-6)](https://ulipolymer.com/polyethyleneimine-cas-9002-98-6/) is a branched polymer composed of repeating ethyleneimine units. Known for its strong cationic charge density, it serves as an excellent adhesion promoter, dispersant, and flocculant. The CAS number 9002-98-6 identifies it as a polymer that combines high reactivity and solubility across a wide range of applications.\nDepending on molecular weight and structure, polyethyleneimine is available in two main types:\n  * **Linear PEI:** Known for its lower viscosity and uniform molecular structure.\n  * **Branched PEI:** Offers higher charge density and excellent reactivity, making it ideal for coatings, adhesives, and chemical modification.\n\n###  ****II********. Key Properties of Polyethyleneimine Solution****\nAqueous polyethyleneimine solution provides a convenient form for industrial use. It is highly alkaline, water-soluble, and reactive with anionic compounds. Typical concentrations range from 30% to 50%, depending on specific customer requirements.\nMain characteristics include:\n  * High cationic charge density\n  * Excellent adhesion to a variety of substrates\n  * Strong chelating and complexing ability\n  * Biodegradability and environmental compatibility\n\nFor users looking for biodegradable polyethyleneimine, our advanced formulations balance performance with eco-safety, supporting sustainable industrial development.\n###  ****III********. Major Applications of Polyethyleneimine****\n####  **1****. Water Treatment**\nOne of the most common uses of [Polyethyleneimine PEI is in water treatment](https://ulipolymer.com/top-uses-of-polyethyleneimine-solution-in-coatings-paper-and-water-treatment/). It functions as a highly efficient flocculant, coagulant, and metal ion chelator.\nPEI removes suspended solids and heavy metals from industrial and municipal wastewater. Due to its strong cationic charge, it bridges negatively charged particles, promoting rapid sedimentation and improved water clarity.\nCompared with conventional coagulants, PEI performs well even under variable pH and temperature conditions, offering long-term cost efficiency. Many environmental engineers prefer biodegradable polyethyleneimine formulations to meet green wastewater treatment standards.\n####  **2****. Coatings and Adhesives**\nIn polyethyleneimine coating applications, PEI serves as a powerful adhesion promoter between polar and non-polar surfaces. It enhances the bonding strength in multilayer coatings, inks, and laminates.\nPEI improves pigment dispersion and print adhesion in paper coatings, and enhances chemical bonding in epoxy and polyurethane adhesives. Its strong film-forming ability makes it suitable for primer formulations, corrosion-resistant coatings, and flexible packaging materials.\nOur polyethyleneimine manufacturer team offers customized grades to achieve optimal performance in coatings that require transparency, flexibility, or conductivity.\n![polyethyleneimine Manufacturer PEI_for_Coatings_and_Adhesives](https://ulipolymer.com/trusted-polyethyleneimine-manufacturer-and-supplier-in-china-quality-meets-innovation/)\n####  **3****. Paper and Pulp Industry**\nPolyethyleneimine (CAS 9002-98-6) has a long history of use in the paper and pulp industry. It acts as a wet-strength resin and retention aid, improving fiber bonding, drainage, and filler retention during papermaking.\nPEI is also used as a surface sizing agent, improving paper smoothness and ink absorbency. Its strong cationic character neutralizes anionic trash and stabilizes process chemistry in recycled paper production.\nWhether for high-speed paper machines or specialty paper grades, Polyethyleneimine PEI helps mills reduce chemical consumption while increasing sheet quality.\n![polyethyleneimine Manufacturer ](https://ulipolymer.com/trusted-polyethyleneimine-manufacturer-and-supplier-in-china-quality-meets-innovation/)\n####  **4****. Oil Production**\nIn the oil and gas industry, PEI plays a vital role as a demulsifier, corrosion inhibitor, and scale control agent. Its strong cationic functionality breaks oil-water emulsions, improving separation efficiency during crude oil processing.\nAdditionally, PEI can be used to modify surfaces in enhanced oil recovery (EOR) systems and as a stabilizer in drilling fluids. Its compatibility with high-salinity environments and temperature stability make it an excellent additive for upstream oilfield applications.\nWhen you buy polyethyleneimine from a trusted supplier, you gain access to tailored grades designed for both onshore and offshore oilfield systems.\n![polyethyleneimine for_Oil_Production](https://ulipolymer.com/trusted-polyethyleneimine-manufacturer-and-supplier-in-china-quality-meets-innovation/)\n####  **5****. Emerging Functional Materials**\nPolyethyleneimine has become a key component in developing emerging functional materials such as nanoparticles, catalysts, and polymer composites.\nIn nanotechnology, PEI acts as a surface modifier to improve dispersion and interfacial bonding of nanoparticles. It can also be used as a reducing agent and stabilizer for metal nanoparticle synthesis.\nModified PEI derivatives are used to create biodegradable polyethyleneimine structures for biomedical and environmental applications. These materials show promise in controlled drug release, biosensors, and smart coatings with self-healing or anti-corrosion properties.\n####  **6****. Semiconductor and Electronic Packaging**\nIn advanced electronics, polyethyleneimine is gaining attention for use in semiconductor surface treatment, photoresist coatings, and electronic packaging materials.\nAs an adhesion promoter, it enhances the interface bonding between metal layers and dielectric substrates. In printed electronics and flexible circuits, Polyethyleneimine PEI improves film conductivity and mechanical strength.\nFurthermore, PEI is used in dielectric modification, electrode passivation, and thin-film encapsulation, supporting high-precision manufacturing in semiconductors and optoelectronics.\n![PEI_for_Semiconductor_and_Electronic](https://ulipolymer.com/trusted-polyethyleneimine-manufacturer-and-supplier-in-china-quality-meets-innovation/)\n###  ****IV********. Why Choose a Reliable Polyethyleneimine Manufacturer?****\nWhen choosing a polyethyleneimine manufacturer, quality consistency, molecular we"

\"Share on Doximity\") * [](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/# \"Print\") * [](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/ \"Reprints\") T he news is everywhere in my social news feeds this morning: A popular fad diet is apparently lethal, scientific research says. Specifically, a study found that caloric restriction, also known as intermittent fasting, has a 91% higher risk of death due to cardiovascular disease. Except scientific research doesn\u2019t say that \u2014 and not only should you not be worried about this study, you shouldn\u2019t be wasting brain glucose thinking about it. Even including that 91% number, which you\u2019ll remember, caused me pain, because I don\u2019t think this result should be remembered. Advertisement The study is a type of nutritional research that is notoriously weak, and right now it\u2019s only available as a [press release](https://newsroom.heart.org/news/8-hour-time-restricted-eating-linked-to-a-91-higher-risk-of-cardiovascular-death). It\u2019s not clear from the many, many news articles on the study whether reporters actually viewed the data that will be presented at an upcoming research meeting held by the American Heart Association. * [](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&via=statnews&text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on X\") * [](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on%20https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/%20via%20@statnews.com \"Share on Bluesky\") * [](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Share on Facebook\") * [](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&mini=true&source=STAT \"Share on LinkedIn\") * [](mailto:?subject=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on&body=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on%0A%0AThe%20news%20is%20everywhere%20today%3A%20Intermittent%20fasting%20can%20apparently%20be%20lethal%2C%20scientific%20research%20says.%20Except%20it%20doesn%27t.%0A%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fwww.statnews.com%2F2024%2F03%2F19%2Fintermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk%2F \"Email\") * [](https://www.doximity.com/inbox/conversations/new_message?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.statnews.com%2F2024%2F03%2F19%2Fintermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk%2F&subject=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on Doximity\") * [](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/# \"Print\") * [](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/ \"Reprints\") ## everywhere in my social news feeds this morning: A popular fad diet is apparently lethal, scientific research says. Specifically, a study found that caloric restriction, also known as intermittent fasting, has a 91% higher risk of death due to cardiovascular disease. Except scientific research doesn\u2019t say that \u2014 and not only should you not be worried about this study, you shouldn\u2019t be wasting brain glucose thinking about it. Even including that 91% number, which you\u2019ll remember, caused me pain, because I don\u2019t think this result should be remembered. Advertisement The study is a type of nutritional research that is notoriously weak, and right now it\u2019s only available as a [press release](https://newsroom.heart.org/news/8-hour-time-restricted-eating-linked-to-a-91-higher-risk-of-cardiovascular-death). It\u2019s not clear from the many, many news articles on the study whether reporters actually viewed the data that will be presented at an upcoming research meeting held by the American Heart Association. * [](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&via=statnews&text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on X\") * [](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on%20https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/%20via%20@statnews.com \"Share on Bluesky\") * [](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Share on Facebook\") * [](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&mini=true&source=STAT \"Share on LinkedIn\") * [](mailto:?subject=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on&body=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on%0A%0AThe%20news%20is%20everywhere%20today%3A%20Intermittent%20fasting%20can%20apparently%20be%20lethal%2C%20scientific%20research%20says.%20Except%20it%20doesn%27t.%0A%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fwww.statnews.com%2F2024%2F03%2F19%2Fintermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk%2F \"Email\") * [](https://www.doximity.com/inbox/conversations/new_message?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.statnews.com%2F2024%2F03%2F19%2Fintermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk%2F&subject=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on Doximity\") * [](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/# \"Print\") * [](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/ \"Reprints\") ## Oh, come on * ![Matthew Herper](https://www.statnews.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/matt-h-avatar-small2-80x80.png) By [Matthew Herper](https://www.statnews.com/staff/matthew-herper/)March 19, 2024 [Reprints](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/) * [ ](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&via=statnews&text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on X\") * [ ](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on%20https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/%20via%20@statnews.com \"Share on Bluesky\") * [ ](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Share on Facebook\") * [ ](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&mini=true&source=STAT \"Share on LinkedIn\") * * [ ](https://www.doximity.com/inbox/conversations/new_message?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.statnews.com%2F2024%2F03%2F19%2Fintermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk%2F&subject=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on Doximity\") * [ ](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Print\") * [ ](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/ \"Reprints\") ![empty wooden round tray with a fork and butter knife as clock hands. -- biotech coverage from STAT](https://www.statnews.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/AdobeStock_261600340-645x645.jpeg) Adobe * [ ](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&via=statnews&text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on X\") * [ ](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on%20https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/%20via%20@statnews.com \"Share on Bluesky\") * [ ](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Share on Facebook\") * [ ](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&mini=true&source=STAT \"Share on LinkedIn\") * * [ ](https://www.doximity.com/inbox/conversations/new_message?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.statnews.com%2F2024%2F03%2F19%2Fintermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk%2F&subject=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on Doximity\") * [ ](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Print\") * [ ](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/ \"Reprints\") The news is everywhere in my social news feeds this morning: A popular fad diet is apparently lethal, scientific research says. Specifically, a study found that caloric restriction, also known as intermittent fasting, has a 91% higher risk of death due to cardiovascular disease. Except scientific research doesn\u2019t say that \u2014 and not only should you not be worried about this study, you shouldn\u2019t be wasting brain glucose thinking about it. Even including that 91% number, which you\u2019ll remember, caused me pain, because I don\u2019t think this result should be remembered. Advertisement The study is a type of nutritional research that is notoriously weak, and right now it\u2019s only available as a [press release](https://newsroom.heart.org/news/8-hour-time-restricted-eating-linked-to-a-91-higher-risk-of-cardiovascular-death). It\u2019s not clear from the many, many news articles on the study whether reporters actually viewed the data that will be presented at an upcoming research meeting held by the American Heart Association. So how am I, a science journalist, confidently dismissing this research? It\u2019s based on observational research, and one lesson from more than 20 years of reporting on health and medicine is that one should be very skeptical of observational research, especially when it is about nutrition. [Newsletters](https://www.statnews.com/signup/) ## Sign up for Morning Rounds Understand how science, health policy, and medicine shape the world every day Please enter a valid email address. [Privacy Policy](https://www.statnews.com/privacy/) In this case, researchers used a really useful research tool, the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES), a survey given to 5,000 people a year about eating and dietary habits, as a starting point. These data were linked by the researchers to a separate database of deaths. Both the survey and the database of deaths are administered by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Advertisement Such databases allow researchers to quickly check to see if dietary choices seem to be associated with health problems. That\u2019s great, because they can help scientists set the direction of more rigorous research that could take years. But the answers that come from doing that are not necessarily reliable. Part of the problem, the easy-to-understand part, is that people answering surveys are not always entirely honest. More than that, especially with food, we often misremember what we\u2019ve eaten and how much. For instance, we might think we followed our diet and totally forget when we slipped up. But the bigger problem is that the people who choose to be on a diet, or those who stay on it, might be fundamentally different from those who don\u2019t in ways that we cannot measure. Perhaps people go on time-restricted diets because they are worried about their health. Perhaps the people who stay on such diets have bodies that work differently than those who can\u2019t fast that long. Perhaps, for whatever reason, the people who were on the diet were different from those who were not simply by random chance. Researchers try to counteract these possibilities by \u201ccontrolling for\u201d the risk factors they know, like body weight and biological sex or gender or age. But the problem is that researchers can only control for the factors they can identify. ## [ Related: The evidence is clear: A liquid-only diet before a colonoscopy is unnecessary ](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/colonoscopy-preparation-liquid-only-diet-data-gastroenterologists/) Let\u2019s look at an example where these phenomena were at play: the decades-long story of whether red wine prevents heart attacks. Originally researchers posited a \u201cFrench paradox\u201d \u2014 that red wine let Parisians down croissants, foie gras, boeuf bourguignon, raclette, and moules frites without the heart attacks the researchers expected because at the time they thought any high-fat diet increased the risk of heart disease. This eventually morphed into the idea that very moderate drinking (no more than a glass of wine a day) had a beneficial effect on heart disease. Except recently some researchers have argued that this apparent benefit isn\u2019t there \u2014 it just looked that way because moderate drinkers were healthier than others in ways researchers had difficulty measuring. The only way to get close to knowing this stuff for sure is to take a large group of people and assign them randomly to, say, drink a glass of red wine a day or be teetotalers. Then you know the two groups of people are probably the same, and if they follow your instructions you can see how red wine makes a difference. Ideally, you would give them either fake wine (a placebo) or real wine so even the participants don\u2019t know what they\u2019re getting. That\u2019s called a blinded randomized controlled trial, and often it makes the \u201cjust-so\u201d stories scientists tell themselves evaporate. For instance, there was an amazing story that Inuit people didn\u2019t get heart disease from high-fat diets because they ate so much fish. This led to many studies, including randomized trials, that seemed to show taking fish oil supplements would reduce heart disease. But higher-quality randomized studies didn\u2019t show this effect \u2014 until a prescription form with a highly purified fish oil did succeed. However, some researchers also doubted that study, because the placebo scientists used might have caused heart attacks. Yes, this is confusing, and that is the point: With nutrition, we need to be really careful about all we don\u2019t know. [UPCOMING EVENT](https://www.statnews.com/events/) ## Connect with today's innovators & tomorrow's thought leaders We're hosting events nationwide (and virtually) to tackle the biggest questions in health and medicine. Browse our upcoming events to see what's on the horizon. [Privacy Policy](https://www.statnews.com/privacy/) Based on an abstract of the new study provided to me by the American Heart Association, which runs the meeting where the results are being presented, it appears the researchers did not ask people if they were following time-restricted diets. What they did was look for people who only ate for a short period of time during the day based on two reports to the survey of what they ate. \u201cWhile informative, this study should be considered exploratory,\u201d said Harlan Krumholz, a leading expert in the science of improving health policy at Yale. \u201cWe are still learnin"

\"Share on Doximity\") * [](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/# \"Print\") * [](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/ \"Reprints\") T he news is everywhere in my social news feeds this morning: A popular fad diet is apparently lethal, scientific research says. Specifically, a study found that caloric restriction, also known as intermittent fasting, has a 91% higher risk of death due to cardiovascular disease. Except scientific research doesn\u2019t say that \u2014 and not only should you not be worried about this study, you shouldn\u2019t be wasting brain glucose thinking about it. Even including that 91% number, which you\u2019ll remember, caused me pain, because I don\u2019t think this result should be remembered. Advertisement The study is a type of nutritional research that is notoriously weak, and right now it\u2019s only available as a [press release](https://newsroom.heart.org/news/8-hour-time-restricted-eating-linked-to-a-91-higher-risk-of-cardiovascular-death). It\u2019s not clear from the many, many news articles on the study whether reporters actually viewed the data that will be presented at an upcoming research meeting held by the American Heart Association. * [](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&via=statnews&text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on X\") * [](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on%20https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/%20via%20@statnews.com \"Share on Bluesky\") * [](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Share on Facebook\") * [](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&mini=true&source=STAT \"Share on LinkedIn\") * [](mailto:?subject=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on&body=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on%0A%0AThe%20news%20is%20everywhere%20today%3A%20Intermittent%20fasting%20can%20apparently%20be%20lethal%2C%20scientific%20research%20says.%20Except%20it%20doesn%27t.%0A%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fwww.statnews.com%2F2024%2F03%2F19%2Fintermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk%2F \"Email\") * [](https://www.doximity.com/inbox/conversations/new_message?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.statnews.com%2F2024%2F03%2F19%2Fintermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk%2F&subject=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on Doximity\") * [](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/# \"Print\") * [](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/ \"Reprints\") ## Oh, come on * ![Matthew Herper](https://www.statnews.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/matt-h-avatar-small2-80x80.png) By [Matthew Herper](https://www.statnews.com/staff/matthew-herper/)March 19, 2024 [Reprints](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/) * [ ](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&via=statnews&text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on X\") * [ ](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on%20https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/%20via%20@statnews.com \"Share on Bluesky\") * [ ](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Share on Facebook\") * [ ](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&mini=true&source=STAT \"Share on LinkedIn\") * * [ ](https://www.doximity.com/inbox/conversations/new_message?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.statnews.com%2F2024%2F03%2F19%2Fintermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk%2F&subject=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on Doximity\") * [ ](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Print\") * [ ](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/ \"Reprints\") ![empty wooden round tray with a fork and butter knife as clock hands. -- biotech coverage from STAT](https://www.statnews.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/AdobeStock_261600340-645x645.jpeg) Adobe * [ ](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&via=statnews&text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on X\") * [ ](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on%20https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/%20via%20@statnews.com \"Share on Bluesky\") * [ ](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Share on Facebook\") * [ ](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/&mini=true&source=STAT \"Share on LinkedIn\") * * [ ](https://www.doximity.com/inbox/conversations/new_message?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.statnews.com%2F2024%2F03%2F19%2Fintermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk%2F&subject=A%20study%20says%20intermittent%20fasting%20is%20making%20people%20drop%20dead.%20Oh%2C%20come%20on \"Share on Doximity\") * [ ](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/intermittent-fasting-study-heart-risk/ \"Print\") * [ ](https://www.parsintl.com/publications/stat/ \"Reprints\") The news is everywhere in my social news feeds this morning: A popular fad diet is apparently lethal, scientific research says. Specifically, a study found that caloric restriction, also known as intermittent fasting, has a 91% higher risk of death due to cardiovascular disease. Except scientific research doesn\u2019t say that \u2014 and not only should you not be worried about this study, you shouldn\u2019t be wasting brain glucose thinking about it. Even including that 91% number, which you\u2019ll remember, caused me pain, because I don\u2019t think this result should be remembered. Advertisement The study is a type of nutritional research that is notoriously weak, and right now it\u2019s only available as a [press release](https://newsroom.heart.org/news/8-hour-time-restricted-eating-linked-to-a-91-higher-risk-of-cardiovascular-death). It\u2019s not clear from the many, many news articles on the study whether reporters actually viewed the data that will be presented at an upcoming research meeting held by the American Heart Association. So how am I, a science journalist, confidently dismissing this research? It\u2019s based on observational research, and one lesson from more than 20 years of reporting on health and medicine is that one should be very skeptical of observational research, especially when it is about nutrition. [Newsletters](https://www.statnews.com/signup/) ## Sign up for Morning Rounds Understand how science, health policy, and medicine shape the world every day Please enter a valid email address. [Privacy Policy](https://www.statnews.com/privacy/) In this case, researchers used a really useful research tool, the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES), a survey given to 5,000 people a year about eating and dietary habits, as a starting point. These data were linked by the researchers to a separate database of deaths. Both the survey and the database of deaths are administered by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Advertisement Such databases allow researchers to quickly check to see if dietary choices seem to be associated with health problems. That\u2019s great, because they can help scientists set the direction of more rigorous research that could take years. But the answers that come from doing that are not necessarily reliable. Part of the problem, the easy-to-understand part, is that people answering surveys are not always entirely honest. More than that, especially with food, we often misremember what we\u2019ve eaten and how much. For instance, we might think we followed our diet and totally forget when we slipped up. But the bigger problem is that the people who choose to be on a diet, or those who stay on it, might be fundamentally different from those who don\u2019t in ways that we cannot measure. Perhaps people go on time-restricted diets because they are worried about their health. Perhaps the people who stay on such diets have bodies that work differently than those who can\u2019t fast that long. Perhaps, for whatever reason, the people who were on the diet were different from those who were not simply by random chance. Researchers try to counteract these possibilities by \u201ccontrolling for\u201d the risk factors they know, like body weight and biological sex or gender or age. But the problem is that researchers can only control for the factors they can identify. ## [ Related: The evidence is clear: A liquid-only diet before a colonoscopy is unnecessary ](https://www.statnews.com/2024/03/19/colonoscopy-preparation-liquid-only-diet-data-gastroenterologists/) Let\u2019s look at an example where these phenomena were at play: the decades-long story of whether red wine prevents heart attacks. Originally researchers posited a \u201cFrench paradox\u201d \u2014 that red wine let Parisians down croissants, foie gras, boeuf bourguignon, raclette, and moules frites without the heart attacks the researchers expected because at the time they thought any high-fat diet increased the risk of heart disease. This eventually morphed into the idea that very moderate drinking (no more than a glass of wine a day) had a beneficial effect on heart disease. Except recently some researchers have argued that this apparent benefit isn\u2019t there \u2014 it just looked that way because moderate drinkers were healthier than others in ways researchers had difficulty measuring. The only way to get close to knowing this stuff for sure is to take a large group of people and assign them randomly to, say, drink a glass of red wine a day or be teetotalers. Then you know the two groups of people are probably the same, and if they follow your instructions you can see how red wine makes a difference. Ideally, you would give them either fake wine (a placebo) or real wine so even the participants don\u2019t know what they\u2019re getting. That\u2019s called a blinded randomized controlled trial, and often it makes the \u201cjust-so\u201d stories scientists tell themselves evaporate. For instance, there was an amazing story that Inuit people didn\u2019t get heart disease from high-fat diets because they ate so much fish. This led to many studies, including randomized trials, that seemed to show taking fish oil supplements would reduce heart disease. But higher-quality randomized studies didn\u2019t show this effect \u2014 until a prescription form with a highly purified fish oil did succeed. However, some researchers also doubted that study, because the placebo scientists used might have caused heart attacks. Yes, this is confusing, and that is the point: With nutrition, we need to be really careful about all we don\u2019t know. [UPCOMING EVENT](https://www.statnews.com/events/) ## Connect with today's innovators & tomorrow's thought leaders We're hosting events nationwide (and virtually) to tackle the biggest questions in health and medicine. Browse our upcoming events to see what's on the horizon. [Privacy Policy](https://www.statnews.com/privacy/) Based on an abstract of the new study provided to me by the American Heart Association, which runs the meeting where the results are being presented, it appears the researchers did not ask people if they were following time-restricted diets. What they did was look for people who only ate for a short period of time during the day based on two reports to the survey of what they ate. \u201cWhile informative, this study should be considered exploratory,\u201d said Harlan Krumholz, a leading expert in the science of improving health policy a"
